Title: Advances and future directions in betalain metabolic engineering

Common_Genes.Gene_Name: Transporters involved in vacuolar sequestration of betalains
Common_Genes.Applied_Species: Plant cells; yeast; microbial or plant cell systems
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Membrane transporter(s), specific family not identified
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Vacuole in plant cells; extracellular medium in yeast
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Proposed to facilitate vacuolar sequestration in plants or secretion from cells, thereby affecting betalain accumulation and productivity
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Not yet identified; proposed future target based on localization/secretion observations
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Physical or chemical permeabilization treatments, oxygen limitation, surfactants/acids, temperature stress, sonification discussed for release from plant cells
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The review highlights that dedicated betalain transporters remain to be identified. 2. Such transporters could enhance secretion in microbial or plant systems or improve vacuolar sequestration in plant cells. 3. Transport engineering is presented as a future direction for increasing yield and simplifying purification.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential targets for secretion engineering and improved pigment storage
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Unknown; altered transport may affect intracellular stability or toxicity
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No specific transporter gene name is provided in the review

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Core betalain pathway; betalamic acid formation branch
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cleaves L-DOPA to form betalamic acid
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms the chromophore precursor required for both betaxanthin and betacyanin formation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Natural colorants; nutritional pigments; potential food, cosmetics, and pharmaceutical applications
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables in vitro/heterologous betaxanthin production when combined with available L-DOPA
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant expression in Escherichia coli; in vitro production of betaxanthins
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Mirabilis jalapa DOD was among the first genes enabling recombinant betaxanthin production in Escherichia coli. 2. It converts L-DOPA to betalamic acid, the central precursor for betalains. 3. Its discovery helped establish the core enzymatic logic required for betalain engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful core pathway gene for reconstructing betalain production in heterologous microbes and plants
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Requires sufficient L-DOPA supply; product profile depends on available amines/cyclo-DOPA
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review notes that in beet, BvDODA1 rather than other DODA homologues is the major contributor to in planta activity

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Yeast; Nicotiana benthamiana; heterologous plants and microbes
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Core betalain pathway; betalamic acid formation branch
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms precursor for both betaxanthin and betacyanin production
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Vacuole in plant cells; released extracellularly in yeast
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Major in planta DOPA-4,5-dioxygenase activity contributor in beet; enables heterologous betaxanthin/betacyanin production with partner enzymes
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: In planta functional assignment in beet; co-expression in yeast and plants; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Core pathway enzyme; not explicitly established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Sometimes paired with L-DOPA or tyrosine feeding depending on host system
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BvDODA1 is identified as the major contributor to DOPA-4,5-dioxygenase activity in beet. 2. Co-expression with CYP76AD enzymes enables heterologous betalain production in yeast and plants. 3. It is a central enzyme for engineering both red and yellow betalain branches.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Essential chassis gene for betalain engineering in crop plants, ornamentals, and microbes
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Output depends on precursor supply and co-expressed CYP activity
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Different beet DODA homologues exist, but BvDODA1 is emphasized as the principal in planta enzyme

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Yeast; Arabidopsis; Nicotiana benthamiana; transgenic plants
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Saccharomyces cerevisiae; Arabidopsis thaliana; Nicotiana benthamiana; NA
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Committed/upstream step plus branch-defining oxidation step
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates tyrosine to L-DOPA and oxidizes L-DOPA to cyclo-DOPA
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool and directly drives flux toward betacyanin formation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables red betalain/betacyanin production; with BvDODA1 and cDOPA5GT is sufficient for betacyanin formation in non-betalain plants
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Co-expression in baker's yeast and plants; heterologous pathway reconstruction; mutational evidence; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Visible pigmentation in engineered seedlings/tissues when expressed, sometimes dependent on tyrosine availability
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: First committed step enzyme and major branch-control enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Tyrosine feeding in some hosts; L-DOPA feeding in early yeast systems
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP76AD1 was a key breakthrough enabling heterologous betalain production. 2. As an α-clade CYP76AD enzyme, it performs both tyrosine hydroxylation and cyclo-DOPA formation. 3. Its activity directs pathway flux toward red betacyanin accumulation. 4. A single amino acid mutation can abolish L-DOPA oxidation while retaining hydroxylase activity, shifting products toward betaxanthins.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for engineering red pigmentation and nutritional betalain enrichment; editing or tuning activity can modulate color palette
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Color output and yield depend on tyrosine availability; may require balancing against precursor competition
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Expression in Arabidopsis with a Portulaca grandiflora DOD yielded visible red pigmentation only with tyrosine supplementation

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates tyrosine to L-DOPA but lacks cyclo-DOPA-forming activity
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool and directs flux predominantly toward betaxanthin production
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Vacuole in plant cells; extracellular release in yeast reported for betalains
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: With BvDODA1 enables yellow betaxanthin production in Nicotiana benthamiana and yeast
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous co-expression in Nicotiana benthamiana and baker's yeast; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP76AD6 is a β-clade CYP76AD enzyme that produces L-DOPA from tyrosine. 2. When combined with BvDODA1, it enables heterologous betaxanthin formation in plants and yeast. 3. It helps access yellow betalain pigmentation by avoiding cyclo-DOPA formation.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Valuable for engineering yellow pigmentation and tuning betalain color output
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Does not support betacyanin formation unless cyclo-DOPA is supplied by another enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review groups CYP76AD5/6/15 as β-clade members with tyrosine hydroxylase activity only

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Glycosylates cyclo-DOPA at the 5-O position, enabling subsequent formation of betanin
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification and direct formation of glucosylated betacyanin betanin
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Red pigment; natural colorant; nutritional pigment
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Together with CYP76AD1 and BvDODA1 is sufficient for betacyanin formation in naturally nonproducing plants
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous co-expression in plants; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. cDOPA5GT is one of three genes sufficient for engineering glucosylated betacyanin formation in non-betalain plants. 2. It glycosylates cyclo-DOPA in an alternative route to betanin. 3. It is a practical downstream module for stable red betalain engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful downstream gene for betanin production in crops and ornamentals
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May yield host-dependent decorated derivatives due to endogenous glycosylation/acylation activities
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review notes an alternative route in which betanidin is glycosylated by betanidin 5-O-glucosyltransferase instead

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Shikimate-derived tyrosine biosynthesis supporting betalain production
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for betalain biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Partially feedback-insensitive isoform that increases precursor supply and caused a sevenfold increase in betalain production when co-expressed with betalain genes in Nicotiana benthamiana
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transient expression with betalain biosynthetic genes; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transiently expressed in Nicotiana benthamiana leaves
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Precursor-supply control point; feedback deregulation highlighted
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BvADHα is a partially tyrosine-insensitive ADH isoform from beet. 2. Co-expression with betalain biosynthetic genes in Nicotiana benthamiana increased betalain production about sevenfold. 3. It enhances betalain yield by increasing tyrosine precursor availability.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising precursor-engineering gene for boosting betalain yield in heterologous systems
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can decrease phenylalanine accumulation and potentially reduce phenylpropanoid-derived metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Presented as an evolutionary adaptation largely adopted by betalain-producing Caryophyllales

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Shikimate pathway supporting tyrosine-derived metabolite production
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes the first step of the shikimate pathway leading to chorismate and downstream tyrosine production
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for tyrosine and tyrosine-derived metabolites including betalains
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Discussed as a strategy to increase tyrosine and tyrosine-derived metabolites in plants, thereby potentially boosting betalain yield
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Referenced expression of feedback-insensitive bacterial AroG in plants
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Upstream control point through feedback deregulation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Feedback-insensitive AroG increases tyrosine and tyrosine-derived metabolites in plants. 2. The review proposes it as a useful upstream engineering strategy to enhance betalain yield. 3. It acts indirectly through precursor supply rather than the core betalain pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential metabolic-engineering tool to elevate precursor flux for betalain production
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May perturb broader aromatic amino acid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned as an engineering strategy rather than as a betalain-specific native gene

Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Positive regulator associated with increased betalain biosynthesis in beet
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene identification as positive regulator in beet (as cited in review)
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Positive regulator of betalain biosynthesis, proposed as a candidate to elevate betalain production
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BvMYB1 is identified as an MYB-type positive regulator of betalain biosynthesis in red beet. 2. The review suggests that TF-based pathway activation could be an alternative engineering route. 3. Heterologous use of BvMYB1 to raise betalains in other betalain-producing species had not yet been reported at the time of publication.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential regulator for boosting endogenous betalain pathways in betalain-producing crops or ornamentals
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Not established; ectopic TF expression may cause pleiotropic effects
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a summarized regulator in this review, not as a newly characterized gene in this article
